perturbation.original_text: The Buyer shall open an irrevocable letter of credit with the bank within 30 days after signing the contract, in favor of the Seller, for 100% value of the total contract value. The letter of credit should state that partial shipments are allowed. The Buyer's agent agrees to pay for the goods in accordance with the actual amount of the goods shipped. 80% of the system value being shipped will be paid against the documents stipulated in Clause 12.1. The remaining 20% of the system value being shipped will be paid against the documents stipulated in Clause 12.2. The Letter of Credit shall be valid until 90 days after the latest shipment is effected.
perturbation.changed_text: The Buyer shall initiate an non-revocable letter of credit with the bank within 60 days after contract signing, in favor of the Seller, for 90% value of the total contract value. The letter of credit should state that partial shipments are not allowed. The Buyer's representative agrees to pay for the goods as per the pro-forma invoice. 70% of the system value being shipped will be paid against the documents stipulated in Clause 12.1. The remaining 30% of the system value being shipped will be paid against the documents stipulated in Clause 12.2. The Letter of Credit shall be valid until 60 days after the latest shipment is effected.
perturbation.explanation: The original text dictates 'irrevocable' L/C to be opened within 30 days for 100% value, allowing partial shipments and payment is based on the amount of goods shipped. The modified text changes this to 'non-revocable' L/C within 60 days for 90% value, disallows partial shipments and payment is based on pro-forma invoice. These changes contradict the payment terms established in relation to shipment and invoice values. The changed terms introduce ambiguity regarding the financial obligations and payment structure between Buyer and Seller. Specifically, the term 'non-revocable' instead of 'irrevocable' introduces uncertainty since typically in trade finance, irrevocable offers greater security.
perturbation.contradicted_text: PAYMENT: Under Letter of Credit or T/T: Under the Letter of Credit: The Buyer shall initiate an non-revocable letter of credit with the bank within 60 days after contract signing, in favor of the Seller, for 90% value of the total contract value. The letter of credit should state that partial shipments are not allowed. The Buyer's representative agrees to pay for the goods as per the pro-forma invoice. 70% of the system value being shipped will be paid against the documents stipulated in Clause 12.1. The remaining 30% of the system value being shipped will be paid against the documents stipulated in Clause 12.2. The Letter of Credit shall be valid until 60 days after the latest shipment is effected. Under the T/T The trustee of the buyer remitted the goods to the seller by telegraphic transfer in batches as agreed upon after signing each order.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces several contradictions. First, it modifies 'irrevocable' to 'non-revocable', which is significant in trade finance. Second, it changes the letter of credit value from 100% to 90%, and disallows partial shipments, conflicting with the original allowance for partial shipments. Third, it bases payment on a 'pro-forma invoice' instead of 'the actual amount of the goods shipped'. These changes directly contradict the payment terms, shipment allowance, and the nature of the L/C itself. These discrepancies create uncertainty and inconsistency regarding the financial obligations between the buyer and seller as originally established, triggering a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Sellers shall mark on each package with fadeless paint the package number, gross weight, net weight, measurements and the wordings: "KEEP AWAY FROM MOISTURE","HANDLE WITH CARE" "THIS SIDE UP" etc. and the shipping mark on each package with fadeless paint.
perturbation.changed_text: The Distributor shall label each package with permanent ink the package identification, total weight, product weight, dimensions and the labels: "KEEP DRY","FRAGILE" "UP" etc. and the cargo identifier on each package with permanent ink.
perturbation.explanation: The original text uses 'Sellers,' 'fadeless paint,' 'package number,' 'gross weight,' 'net weight,' 'measurements,' and specific wordings for package marking. The modified text uses 'Distributor,' 'permanent ink,' 'package identification,' 'total weight,' 'product weight,' 'dimensions,' and different wordings for package marking. By changing these terms without explicit definition, it's unclear whether the 'Sellers' and 'Distributor' are the same entity and whether the labeling requirements are consistent. The difference in labeling requirements and instructions introduces an In-Text Contradiction, specially on who is being refered since if they are the same party, the marking difference can be confusing.
perturbation.contradicted_text: SHIPPING MARK: The Distributor shall label each package with permanent ink the package identification, total weight, product weight, dimensions and the labels: "KEEP DRY","FRAGILE" "UP" etc. and the cargo identifier on each package with permanent ink.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies requirements for the 'Sellers' using 'fadeless paint,' while the changed text applies different requirements to the 'Distributor' using 'permanent ink'. The contradictory text also says 'The Distributor shall label each package with permanent ink the package identification, total weight, product weight, dimensions and the labels: "KEEP DRY","FRAGILE" "UP" etc. and the cargo identifier on each package with permanent ink.' making it an In-Text Contradiction based on Misaligned Terminology, as the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations, as it is unclear who is responsible for what type of labeling, creating a direct conflict and uncertainty about the obligations of each party.

perturbation.original_text: The Sellers guarantee that the commodity hereof is complies in all respects with the quality and specification stipulated in this Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: The Vendor warrants that the merchandise herein meets all quality standards and requirements outlined elsewhere.
perturbation.explanation: The original text uses 'Sellers' and 'commodity,' guaranteeing compliance with the 'quality and specification stipulated in this Contract.' The modified text uses 'Vendor' and 'merchandise,' warranting compliance with 'all quality standards and requirements outlined elsewhere.' This introduces an In-Text Contradiction because it is unclear if 'Sellers' and 'Vendor' are the same entity. The changed text references quality standards 'outlined elsewhere,' potentially conflicting with the defined specifications in the original contract, by introducing this in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: GUARANTEE OF QUALITY: The Vendor warrants that the merchandise herein meets all quality standards and requirements outlined elsewhere.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'Sellers' with 'Vendor' and 'commodity' with 'merchandise,' while also referencing 'quality standards and requirements outlined elsewhere' instead of the 'quality and specification stipulated in this Contract.' This creates a potential conflict. The contradicted text, which is the same as the Changed Text, warrants that the merchandise meets all quality standards outlined elsewhere. Because the Contradicted Text is the same as the Changed Text and both refer to requirements 'outlined elsewhere,' there is an in-text contradiction as to which quality standards or specifications the 'Vendor' or 'Sellers' must adhere to, especially if 'Sellers' and 'Vendor' are not the same entity and the standards 'outlined elsewhere' differ from those 'stipulated in this Contract.'
